name: Total Cholesterol
description: Total amount of cholesterol in the blood including HDL, LDL, and VLDL.
why_it_matters: Cholesterol is essential for building cells and producing hormones, but excess levels can lead to plaque buildup in arteries, increasing cardiovascular disease risk.
relationships: Closely linked to LDL-C, HDL-C, Triglycerides, and ApoB. When Total Cholesterol is elevated, check the LDL/HDL ratio and ApoB for a clearer cardiovascular risk picture. High triglycerides can inflate total cholesterol without proportional LDL increases.
how_to_improve: Increase soluble fiber intake (oats, beans, lentils). Add omega-3 rich foods (salmon, walnuts). Replace saturated fats with monounsaturated fats (olive oil, avocados). Exercise 150+ minutes per week. Consider plant sterols/stanols.

name: HDL Cholesterol
description: High-density lipoprotein that removes cholesterol from arteries and transports it to the liver for disposal.
why_it_matters: HDL acts as a protective scavenger, clearing excess cholesterol from the bloodstream. Higher HDL is associated with lower cardiovascular risk and better endothelial function.
relationships: Inversely related to Triglycerides — high triglycerides often accompany low HDL. Works with ApoB and LDL-C to determine overall cardiovascular risk. SHBG levels can correlate with HDL in hormonal contexts.
how_to_improve: Regular aerobic exercise (strongest modifier). Consume healthy fats (olive oil, nuts, fatty fish). Moderate alcohol intake may raise HDL. Quit smoking. Lose excess body fat. Add niacin-rich foods.

name: LDL Cholesterol
description: Low-density lipoprotein that carries cholesterol to tissues. Often called 'bad cholesterol' when elevated.
why_it_matters: LDL particles can penetrate arterial walls and trigger plaque formation. Elevated LDL is the primary modifiable risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.
relationships: Directly correlates with ApoB (each LDL particle carries one ApoB). When LDL is borderline, ApoB provides a more accurate particle count. High Triglycerides can make calculated LDL less accurate (use direct LDL or ApoB instead).
how_to_improve: Reduce saturated fat and trans fat intake. Increase soluble fiber (psyllium, oats). Add plant sterols. Exercise regularly. Maintain healthy weight. Consider Mediterranean diet pattern.

name: Triglycerides
description: The most common type of fat in the blood, stored from excess calories and released for energy between meals.
why_it_matters: Elevated triglycerides independently increase cardiovascular risk, contribute to fatty liver, and are a hallmark of metabolic syndrome. They also indicate how well the body processes dietary carbohydrates and fats.
relationships: Inversely correlated with HDL — high triglycerides usually mean low HDL. Strongly influenced by Insulin and Glucose levels. Elevated triglycerides make calculated LDL less reliable. Connected to Non-HDL Cholesterol.
how_to_improve: Reduce refined carbohydrates and added sugars. Limit alcohol. Increase omega-3 fatty acids (fatty fish, fish oil). Exercise regularly. Lose excess weight. Avoid large, carb-heavy meals.

name: Non-HDL Cholesterol
description: Total cholesterol minus HDL, capturing all atherogenic (plaque-forming) cholesterol particles.
why_it_matters: Non-HDL cholesterol is a better predictor of cardiovascular risk than LDL alone because it includes VLDL and Lp(a). It remains accurate even after eating, unlike fasting LDL measurements.
relationships: Includes LDL-C plus VLDL (triglyceride-rich particles) and Lp(a). When Triglycerides are elevated, Non-HDL is more reliable than LDL alone. Correlates strongly with ApoB.
how_to_improve: Same strategies as LDL reduction: reduce saturated fat, increase fiber, exercise, maintain healthy weight. Also address triglycerides (reduce sugar/alcohol) since VLDL is included.

name: Apolipoprotein B
description: A protein found on every atherogenic lipoprotein particle (LDL, VLDL, Lp(a)). One ApoB per particle.
why_it_matters: ApoB directly counts the number of atherogenic particles in the blood, making it arguably the best single marker for cardiovascular risk — better than LDL-C, which measures cholesterol mass rather than particle count.
relationships: One-to-one with LDL particle number. When ApoB is high but LDL-C is normal, you have many small dense LDL particles (higher risk). Correlates with Lp(a), Non-HDL Cholesterol, and Triglycerides.
how_to_improve: Reduce saturated fat intake. Exercise consistently. Maintain healthy weight. Increase soluble fiber. Mediterranean diet pattern. If lifestyle insufficient, discuss statins with physician.

name: Lipoprotein(a)
description: A genetically determined LDL-like particle with an additional apolipoprotein(a) protein that promotes clotting and inflammation.
why_it_matters: Elevated Lp(a) is an independent, largely genetic risk factor for heart attack and stroke. Unlike LDL, it is minimally affected by diet and exercise, making it important to measure at least once.
relationships: Adds to the atherogenic burden measured by ApoB and Non-HDL Cholesterol. Can cause LDL-C to appear elevated. Not reduced by statins. High Lp(a) combined with high LDL-C or ApoB compounds cardiovascular risk significantly.
how_to_improve: Largely genetic and resistant to lifestyle changes. Niacin (under medical supervision) may reduce levels modestly. PCSK9 inhibitors can lower Lp(a) ~25%. New antisense therapies in clinical trials. Focus on aggressively optimizing other modifiable risk factors.

name: Glucose (Fasting)
description: Blood sugar level after fasting 8-12 hours, reflecting baseline glucose regulation.
why_it_matters: Fasting glucose is an early indicator of insulin resistance and diabetes risk. Chronically elevated glucose damages blood vessels, nerves, and organs, accelerating aging and disease.
relationships: Directly linked to Insulin and HOMA-IR (insulin resistance). Correlates with HbA1c (3-month glucose average). High fasting glucose with high insulin suggests insulin resistance. Triglycerides often rise in parallel.
how_to_improve: Reduce refined carbohydrates and added sugars. Prioritize fiber, protein, and healthy fats. Exercise regularly (especially post-meal walks). Improve sleep quality. Manage stress. Consider time-restricted eating.

name: HbA1c
description: Glycated hemoglobin reflecting average blood sugar levels over the past 2-3 months.
why_it_matters: HbA1c provides a long-term view of glucose control, unaffected by day-to-day fluctuations. It is the gold standard for diagnosing and monitoring diabetes and prediabetes.
relationships: Directly correlates with average Glucose levels. Complements fasting Glucose and Insulin for a complete metabolic picture. Anemia or hemoglobin variants (check MCV, RDW) can falsely alter HbA1c readings.
how_to_improve: Same strategies as fasting glucose: reduce glycemic load, increase fiber and protein, exercise regularly, improve sleep, manage stress. Consistent improvements over 2-3 months will lower HbA1c.

name: Insulin (Fasting)
description: Hormone produced by the pancreas that regulates blood sugar by facilitating glucose uptake into cells.
why_it_matters: Fasting insulin is one of the earliest markers of metabolic dysfunction. Elevated insulin (even with normal glucose) indicates insulin resistance years before diabetes develops.
relationships: Used with fasting Glucose to calculate HOMA-IR. Elevated insulin drives Triglyceride production and lowers HDL. Connected to SHBG (high insulin suppresses SHBG). Influences Testosterone levels in both sexes.
how_to_improve: Reduce refined carbs and sugar. Resistance training (increases insulin sensitivity). High-intensity interval training. Improve sleep (poor sleep worsens insulin resistance). Increase muscle mass. Consider intermittent fasting.

name: HOMA-IR
description: Homeostatic Model Assessment of Insulin Resistance — a calculated index from fasting glucose and insulin.
why_it_matters: HOMA-IR quantifies insulin resistance, the root driver of metabolic syndrome, type 2 diabetes, NAFLD, and increased cardiovascular risk. It detects metabolic dysfunction earlier than glucose or HbA1c alone.
relationships: Calculated from fasting Glucose and Insulin: (Glucose x Insulin) / 405. Correlates with Triglycerides, HDL (inversely), and visceral fat. High HOMA-IR predicts future diabetes and cardiovascular events.
how_to_improve: All insulin-sensitizing strategies: exercise (both aerobic and resistance), reduce refined carbs, improve sleep, reduce visceral fat, manage stress. Berberine and magnesium may help as supplements.

name: Testosterone (Total)
description: The primary male sex hormone, also important in females at lower levels. Includes both bound and free forms.
why_it_matters: Testosterone drives muscle growth, bone density, energy, mood, libido, and recovery. Low levels impair athletic performance, increase body fat, and reduce motivation and cognitive function.
relationships: SHBG binds testosterone, so high SHBG can lower free testosterone even when total is normal. Check Free Testosterone and SHBG together. Estradiol is produced from testosterone via aromatase. LH and FSH from the pituitary drive testosterone production.
how_to_improve: Prioritize sleep (7-9 hours). Resistance training with compound movements. Maintain healthy body fat (15-20%). Reduce alcohol. Manage stress (cortisol suppresses testosterone). Ensure adequate zinc, vitamin D, and magnesium.

name: Free Testosterone
description: The unbound, biologically active form of testosterone available for use by tissues.
why_it_matters: Free testosterone is what your body can actually use. Even with normal total testosterone, low free testosterone (often from high SHBG) causes symptoms of deficiency: fatigue, muscle loss, low libido.
relationships: Determined by Total Testosterone and SHBG levels. High SHBG traps more testosterone, lowering free levels. Albumin also binds testosterone loosely. Elevated Insulin can suppress SHBG, paradoxically raising free testosterone.
how_to_improve: Same as total testosterone optimization. If SHBG is very high, address underlying causes (hyperthyroidism, liver disease, estrogen excess). Boron supplementation (6-10mg/day) may modestly lower SHBG.

name: Estradiol
description: The primary and most potent form of estrogen, important for bone health, cardiovascular protection, and brain function in both sexes.
why_it_matters: In men, estradiol is needed for bone density, libido, and cardiovascular protection, but excess causes gynecomastia and water retention. In women, it regulates the menstrual cycle and supports bone and heart health.
relationships: Produced from Testosterone via aromatase enzyme. High body fat increases aromatase activity, raising Estradiol. Inversely related to FSH and LH in feedback loops. SHBG binds estradiol, affecting free levels.
how_to_improve: Maintain healthy body fat (excess fat increases aromatase). Cruciferous vegetables support estrogen metabolism. Limit alcohol (increases aromatase). Ensure adequate fiber for estrogen clearance. Exercise regularly.

name: Sex Hormone Binding Globulin
description: A liver-produced protein that binds sex hormones (testosterone, estradiol), regulating how much is freely available to tissues.
why_it_matters: SHBG is the gatekeeper of hormone bioavailability. Too high and free testosterone drops despite normal total levels. Too low (common with insulin resistance) and hormone balance is disrupted.
relationships: Inversely related to Insulin (insulin resistance lowers SHBG). Determines Free Testosterone from Total Testosterone. Thyroid hormones increase SHBG. Obesity and metabolic syndrome lower it.
how_to_improve: If too low: address insulin resistance, lose visceral fat, reduce simple carbs. If too high: investigate thyroid function, liver health, and estrogen levels. Exercise and healthy weight normalize SHBG in most cases.

name: Cortisol
description: The primary stress hormone produced by the adrenal glands, following a diurnal pattern with highest levels in the morning.
why_it_matters: Cortisol regulates metabolism, immune function, and the stress response. Chronically elevated cortisol impairs recovery, breaks down muscle, increases abdominal fat, disrupts sleep, and suppresses immune function.
relationships: Inversely affects Testosterone production. Elevated cortisol raises blood Glucose and Insulin. Chronic high cortisol can suppress Thyroid function (lower T3). Impacts HRV and recovery scores negatively.
how_to_improve: Improve sleep quality and duration. Practice stress management (meditation, breathwork). Avoid overtraining — ensure adequate recovery. Reduce caffeine after noon. Ashwagandha has evidence for cortisol reduction. Prioritize social connection.

name: DHEA-S
description: Dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate, an adrenal hormone and precursor to both testosterone and estrogen.
why_it_matters: DHEA-S is the most abundant steroid hormone and a key precursor for sex hormones. It declines with age and is considered a biomarker of biological aging, immune function, and vitality.
relationships: Precursor to Testosterone and Estradiol. Produced by the same adrenal pathway as Cortisol. Chronic stress can shift production toward cortisol and away from DHEA-S. Correlates with overall hormonal health.
how_to_improve: Manage stress (chronic stress depletes DHEA-S). Exercise regularly. Ensure adequate sleep. DHEA supplementation under medical guidance. Healthy fats support hormone production.

name: Follicle Stimulating Hormone
description: A pituitary hormone that stimulates sperm production in men and egg development in women.
why_it_matters: FSH reflects the health of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis. Elevated FSH can indicate primary gonadal failure, while low FSH may suggest pituitary issues or suppression from exogenous hormones.
relationships: Works with LH to regulate gonadal function. High FSH with low Testosterone suggests primary hypogonadism. Low FSH with low Testosterone suggests secondary (pituitary) hypogonadism. Inversely related to Inhibin B.
how_to_improve: FSH is typically a diagnostic marker rather than a direct optimization target. Address underlying causes: if elevated, investigate gonadal health; if suppressed, check for exogenous hormone use or pituitary issues.

name: Luteinizing Hormone
description: A pituitary hormone that triggers testosterone production in men and ovulation in women.
why_it_matters: LH is the primary signal telling the testes to produce testosterone. Abnormal LH helps distinguish between primary (testicular) and secondary (pituitary) causes of low testosterone.
relationships: Directly stimulates Testosterone production. Works in tandem with FSH. High LH + low Testosterone = primary hypogonadism. Low LH + low Testosterone = secondary hypogonadism. Pulsatile secretion makes single measurements variable.
how_to_improve: LH is a diagnostic marker. Optimize the HPG axis by: ensuring adequate sleep, managing stress, maintaining healthy body fat, avoiding endocrine disruptors, and supporting overall pituitary health.

name: TSH
description: Thyroid Stimulating Hormone from the pituitary, the primary screening test for thyroid function.
why_it_matters: TSH is the most sensitive marker for thyroid dysfunction. Even subtle thyroid imbalances affect metabolism, energy, heart rate, body temperature, weight, mood, and recovery from exercise.
relationships: Inversely related to Free T4 and Free T3 in a feedback loop. High TSH suggests hypothyroidism (low thyroid). Low TSH suggests hyperthyroidism. Cortisol and Insulin can both influence TSH levels.
how_to_improve: Ensure adequate iodine, selenium, and zinc. Manage stress (high cortisol can suppress TSH). Avoid excessive soy and cruciferous vegetables if thyroid is borderline. Support gut health (autoimmune thyroid often has gut connection).

name: Free T4
description: The unbound form of thyroxine, the main hormone produced by the thyroid gland.
why_it_matters: Free T4 is the thyroid's primary output and the reservoir for conversion to active T3. Low Free T4 with high TSH confirms hypothyroidism. It reflects the thyroid gland's actual hormone production capacity.
relationships: Converted to Free T3 (the active form) in peripheral tissues. TSH stimulates T4 production. Selenium is required for T4-to-T3 conversion. Cortisol and illness can impair conversion. Check alongside TSH and Free T3 for complete picture.
how_to_improve: Support thyroid hormone production: adequate iodine (seaweed, iodized salt), selenium (Brazil nuts), zinc. Reduce inflammation. Manage stress. If hypothyroid, levothyroxine is the standard treatment.

name: Free T3
description: The unbound, biologically active thyroid hormone that drives metabolism at the cellular level.
why_it_matters: Free T3 is the active thyroid hormone that cells actually use. Even with normal TSH and T4, low T3 (poor conversion) causes hypothyroid symptoms: fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, brain fog, poor recovery.
relationships: Converted from Free T4 by deiodinase enzymes (selenium-dependent). Low T3 with normal T4 suggests conversion issues (stress, inflammation, selenium deficiency). Cortisol and calorie restriction can reduce T4-to-T3 conversion.
how_to_improve: Selenium supplementation (200mcg/day). Adequate calories (severe restriction drops T3). Manage stress and inflammation. Zinc and iron support conversion. Avoid extreme dieting.

name: Vitamin D (25-OH)
description: The storage form of vitamin D measured in blood, reflecting overall vitamin D status from sun exposure, diet, and supplements.
why_it_matters: Vitamin D is actually a hormone precursor affecting 1000+ genes. Deficiency is linked to weakened immunity, poor bone health, depression, impaired recovery, and increased injury risk in athletes.
relationships: Works with Calcium and Parathyroid Hormone (PTH) for bone health. Supports immune function (impacts inflammation markers like hs-CRP). Influences Testosterone production. Deficiency common with low sun exposure and darker skin tones.
how_to_improve: Sun exposure (10-30 min midday). Supplement D3 (2000-5000 IU/day with fat for absorption). Fatty fish, egg yolks, fortified foods. Test and adjust dose — response varies widely. Take with K2 for bone mineral direction.

name: Vitamin B12
description: Essential water-soluble vitamin for nerve function, red blood cell formation, and DNA synthesis.
why_it_matters: B12 deficiency causes fatigue, weakness, neurological symptoms (tingling, balance issues), cognitive decline, and megaloblastic anemia. Common in vegetarians, older adults, and those on metformin or PPIs.
relationships: Low B12 causes elevated MCV (large red blood cells) and potentially high Homocysteine. Works with Folate for DNA synthesis — deficiency in either causes similar anemia. Check alongside Iron, Ferritin, and Folate when investigating fatigue.
how_to_improve: Meat, fish, eggs, dairy are best sources. Supplementation: methylcobalamin or hydroxocobalamin (better absorbed than cyanocobalamin). Sublingual or injection if absorption is impaired. Vegans need supplementation.

name: Folate
description: A B-vitamin (B9) essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and methylation reactions.
why_it_matters: Folate is critical for cell growth and repair, especially during periods of rapid cell division. Deficiency impairs recovery, causes megaloblastic anemia, and raises homocysteine (cardiovascular risk).
relationships: Works closely with Vitamin B12 — deficiency in either causes megaloblastic anemia (elevated MCV). Both are needed to keep Homocysteine low. MTHFR gene variants can impair folate metabolism.
how_to_improve: Leafy greens, legumes, fortified grains. Supplement with methylfolate (5-MTHF) rather than folic acid, especially with MTHFR variants. 400-800mcg/day is typical. Avoid excessive alcohol (depletes folate).

name: Iron
description: A mineral essential for oxygen transport in hemoglobin and energy production in mitochondria.
why_it_matters: Iron is central to oxygen delivery and energy metabolism. Deficiency causes fatigue, weakness, impaired exercise capacity, and cognitive dysfunction. Excess iron causes oxidative damage to organs.
relationships: Works with Ferritin (storage), Iron Saturation (transport efficiency), Hemoglobin (functional iron), and TIBC. Low iron with low Ferritin = iron deficiency. High iron with high Ferritin = possible iron overload (hemochromatosis).
how_to_improve: If low: red meat, organ meats, spinach, legumes. Take with vitamin C for absorption. Avoid tea/coffee with iron-rich meals. If high: donate blood, reduce red meat, avoid iron supplements and vitamin C with meals.

name: Ferritin
description: Iron-storage protein reflecting body iron reserves. Also an acute-phase reactant that rises with inflammation.
why_it_matters: Ferritin is the best single test for iron stores. Low ferritin causes fatigue, hair loss, restless legs, and impaired athletic performance long before frank anemia develops. Very high ferritin may indicate iron overload or inflammation.
relationships: Low Ferritin with low Hemoglobin and high RDW points toward iron-deficiency anemia. Ferritin can be falsely elevated by inflammation (check hs-CRP). Works with Iron Saturation and Hemoglobin for complete iron picture.
how_to_improve: If low: iron-rich foods (red meat, liver, lentils) with vitamin C. Iron bisglycinate supplements are well-tolerated. If high: rule out inflammation first, then consider blood donation. Athletes and menstruating women need more iron.

name: Iron % Saturation
description: Percentage of transferrin (iron transport protein) that is saturated with iron, reflecting iron availability.
why_it_matters: Iron saturation shows how efficiently iron is being transported and made available to tissues. Low saturation means iron supply is inadequate even if stores (ferritin) haven't fully depleted yet. High saturation may indicate iron overload.
relationships: Calculated from serum Iron and TIBC. Low saturation with low Ferritin = iron deficiency. High saturation with high Ferritin = possible hemochromatosis. Inflammation can lower saturation even with adequate stores.
how_to_improve: Same as iron optimization. If low: increase dietary iron with vitamin C. If consistently >45%, investigate hereditary hemochromatosis (HFE gene test). Therapeutic phlebotomy for iron overload.

name: Calcium
description: Essential mineral for bone health, muscle contraction, nerve signaling, and blood clotting.
why_it_matters: Blood calcium is tightly regulated — even small deviations signal significant problems. High calcium can indicate hyperparathyroidism or malignancy. Low calcium affects muscle function, heart rhythm, and bone density.
relationships: Regulated by PTH, Vitamin D, and Albumin. Always interpret with Albumin (low albumin falsely lowers calcium). Vitamin D deficiency causes secondary hyperparathyroidism, raising PTH to maintain calcium.
how_to_improve: Dairy, leafy greens, fortified foods. Ensure adequate Vitamin D for absorption. Weight-bearing exercise supports calcium utilization. If high, investigate PTH and kidney function.

name: Magnesium
description: Essential mineral involved in 300+ enzymatic reactions, crucial for muscle function, sleep, and energy production.
why_it_matters: Magnesium deficiency is extremely common and affects sleep quality, muscle recovery, insulin sensitivity, blood pressure, and stress resilience. Serum levels only reflect 1% of body stores, so deficiency is underdiagnosed.
relationships: Works with Calcium for muscle contraction/relaxation. Supports insulin sensitivity (low magnesium worsens insulin resistance). Influences Vitamin D metabolism. Low magnesium can elevate blood pressure and worsen sleep quality.
how_to_improve: Dark leafy greens, nuts, seeds, dark chocolate, legumes. Supplement with magnesium glycinate (best for sleep/relaxation) or magnesium threonate (brain). 200-400mg/day elemental magnesium. RBC magnesium is a better test than serum.

name: Zinc
description: Trace mineral essential for immune function, testosterone production, wound healing, and taste/smell.
why_it_matters: Zinc is critical for immune defense, hormone production (especially testosterone), protein synthesis, and wound healing. Deficiency impairs recovery, weakens immunity, and can reduce testosterone levels.
relationships: Required for testosterone synthesis — low zinc directly lowers Testosterone. Competes with Copper for absorption (excess zinc can cause copper deficiency). Supports thyroid hormone conversion (T4 to T3). Important for immune markers.
how_to_improve: Oysters, red meat, poultry, beans, nuts, seeds. Supplement with zinc picolinate or glycinate (15-30mg/day). Take away from iron and calcium for better absorption. Balance with copper if supplementing long-term.

name: hs-CRP
description: High-sensitivity C-reactive protein, a marker of systemic inflammation produced by the liver.
why_it_matters: hs-CRP is the best single marker for chronic low-grade inflammation, which drives cardiovascular disease, cancer, neurodegeneration, and accelerated aging. It independently predicts heart attack risk beyond cholesterol.
relationships: Elevated by infections, obesity, poor sleep, stress, and gut dysfunction. Can falsely elevate Ferritin. Correlates with Insulin resistance and Triglycerides. When elevated alongside ApoB, cardiovascular risk multiplies.
how_to_improve: Anti-inflammatory diet (Mediterranean pattern, omega-3s). Exercise regularly. Optimize sleep. Reduce visceral fat. Manage stress. Limit ultra-processed foods. Address dental health. Curcumin and omega-3 supplements may help.

name: Homocysteine
description: An amino acid intermediate in methionine metabolism. Elevated levels indicate impaired methylation.
why_it_matters: High homocysteine damages blood vessel walls, increases blood clot risk, and is associated with cardiovascular disease, stroke, and cognitive decline. It reflects the efficiency of methylation pathways critical for DNA repair.
relationships: Elevated by deficiencies in Vitamin B12, Folate, and B6 — these are required cofactors for homocysteine metabolism. MTHFR gene variants impair folate processing, raising homocysteine. Kidney function (eGFR, Creatinine) also affects clearance.
how_to_improve: Supplement B12, folate (methylfolate if MTHFR+), and B6. Reduce methionine-heavy foods if very high. Betaine (TMG) is an alternative methyl donor. Address kidney function if impaired.

name: Hemoglobin
description: The iron-containing protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from lungs to tissues and returns carbon dioxide.
why_it_matters: Hemoglobin directly determines oxygen-carrying capacity. Low hemoglobin (anemia) causes fatigue, weakness, impaired exercise performance, and cognitive dysfunction. Athletes need optimal hemoglobin for peak performance.
relationships: Depends on adequate Iron, Ferritin, Vitamin B12, and Folate. Low hemoglobin with low MCV = iron deficiency. Low hemoglobin with high MCV = B12/folate deficiency. Check with Hematocrit, RDW, and iron panel for complete picture.
how_to_improve: Address the underlying cause: iron supplementation for iron-deficiency anemia, B12/folate for megaloblastic anemia. Ensure adequate protein intake. Altitude training naturally stimulates hemoglobin production. Avoid blood donation if borderline low.

name: Hematocrit
description: Percentage of blood volume occupied by red blood cells.
why_it_matters: Hematocrit reflects red blood cell mass and hydration status. Low values indicate anemia; high values increase blood viscosity and clotting risk. Dehydration can falsely elevate hematocrit.
relationships: Moves in parallel with Hemoglobin. Dehydration increases hematocrit without actual red cell changes. High hematocrit can occur with testosterone therapy, sleep apnea, or polycythemia. Low hematocrit mirrors anemia causes.
how_to_improve: If low: address anemia causes (iron, B12, folate). If high: ensure adequate hydration, investigate secondary causes (sleep apnea, testosterone use). Regular blood donation if consistently elevated.

name: Red Blood Cell Count
description: Total number of red blood cells per microliter of blood.
why_it_matters: Red blood cells are the oxygen delivery system. Abnormal counts affect oxygen transport, exercise capacity, and overall energy. Both high and low counts have clinical significance.
relationships: Works with Hemoglobin and Hematocrit to assess oxygen-carrying capacity. MCV, MCH, and MCHC describe the size and hemoglobin content of individual red cells. RDW measures variation in red cell size.
how_to_improve: If low: address nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12, folate), check for chronic disease or blood loss. If high: ensure hydration, check for sleep apnea, altitude effects, or polycythemia vera.

name: Mean Corpuscular Volume
description: Average size of red blood cells. Key for classifying the type of anemia.
why_it_matters: MCV categorizes anemias: low MCV (microcytic) suggests iron deficiency, high MCV (macrocytic) suggests B12 or folate deficiency. Even without anemia, abnormal MCV signals nutritional imbalances.
relationships: Low MCV with low Ferritin = iron deficiency anemia. High MCV with low B12 or Folate = megaloblastic anemia. Alcohol excess raises MCV. Thyroid dysfunction can also affect MCV. Check alongside RDW and reticulocyte count.
how_to_improve: If low: iron supplementation. If high: B12 and/or folate supplementation. Reduce alcohol if elevated. Address thyroid dysfunction if present.

name: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin
description: Average amount of hemoglobin in each red blood cell.
why_it_matters: MCH helps determine whether red blood cells are carrying adequate hemoglobin. Low MCH parallels iron deficiency. High MCH accompanies B12/folate deficiency.
relationships: Moves in parallel with MCV — low MCH with low MCV = iron deficiency, high MCH with high MCV = B12/folate deficiency. Complements MCHC for characterizing red cell hemoglobin content.
how_to_improve: Same as MCV: address iron deficiency (if low) or B12/folate deficiency (if high).

name: Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration
description: Average concentration of hemoglobin within each red blood cell.
why_it_matters: MCHC detects whether hemoglobin is adequately concentrated in red cells. Low MCHC (hypochromic cells) is a hallmark of iron deficiency. High MCHC can indicate spherocytosis or laboratory error.
relationships: Low MCHC with low Ferritin and low MCV strongly confirms iron deficiency. Calculated from Hemoglobin/Hematocrit. Complements MCV and MCH for complete red cell characterization.
how_to_improve: If low: iron supplementation is the primary intervention. Pair with vitamin C for absorption. If high: investigate hemolytic conditions.

name: Red Cell Distribution Width
description: Measures variation in red blood cell size (anisocytosis). Higher values indicate more size variability.
why_it_matters: Elevated RDW is an early marker of nutritional deficiency (iron, B12, folate) even before anemia develops. It is also an independent predictor of mortality and cardiovascular risk in the general population.
relationships: High RDW with low MCV = iron deficiency. High RDW with high MCV = B12/folate deficiency. High RDW with normal MCV can indicate mixed deficiency or early deficiency. Correlates with inflammation (hs-CRP).
how_to_improve: Identify and correct underlying nutritional deficiency (iron, B12, folate). Address chronic inflammation. RDW improves as the deficiency is corrected and new uniform red cells are produced (takes 2-3 months).

name: Platelets
description: Cell fragments responsible for blood clotting and wound healing.
why_it_matters: Platelets are essential for hemostasis. Low counts increase bleeding risk. High counts can indicate inflammation, iron deficiency, or myeloproliferative disorders. Extreme values require medical evaluation.
relationships: Can be elevated by inflammation (reactive thrombocytosis) or iron deficiency. Low platelets can occur with liver disease (check ALT, AST), autoimmune conditions, or bone marrow disorders.
how_to_improve: Platelets are typically a diagnostic marker. If low: investigate underlying causes (infections, autoimmune, medications). If high: address inflammation or iron deficiency driving reactive elevation.

name: White Blood Cell Count
description: Total number of immune cells in the blood, including neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, and basophils.
why_it_matters: WBC count reflects immune system activity. Elevated counts suggest infection, inflammation, or stress. Chronically elevated WBC is associated with increased cardiovascular risk and mortality.
relationships: Made up of Neutrophils (50-70%), Lymphocytes (20-40%), Monocytes (2-8%), Eosinophils (1-4%), and Basophils (<1%). Elevated WBC correlates with hs-CRP and inflammation. Cortisol (stress) can elevate neutrophils.
how_to_improve: If elevated without infection: reduce inflammation (anti-inflammatory diet, exercise, sleep, stress management). If chronically high, investigate underlying causes. Low WBC may indicate viral infection or immune suppression.

name: Neutrophils
description: The most abundant white blood cell type, first responders to bacterial infections and tissue damage.
why_it_matters: Neutrophils are the immune system's rapid-response force. Elevated neutrophils indicate acute infection, inflammation, or physiologic stress. Low neutrophils (neutropenia) increase infection susceptibility.
relationships: Elevated by Cortisol (stress response), bacterial infections, and intense exercise. The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio is a useful inflammation marker. Check alongside WBC, hs-CRP, and Lymphocytes.
how_to_improve: If elevated: address infection or inflammation, manage stress, ensure recovery from intense training. If low: investigate medications, viral infections, or autoimmune causes.

name: Lymphocytes
description: White blood cells (T-cells, B-cells, NK cells) that drive adaptive immunity and long-term immune memory.
why_it_matters: Lymphocytes are the adaptive immune system — they fight viral infections, produce antibodies, and provide immune memory. Low lymphocytes indicate immune suppression, often from overtraining, chronic stress, or illness.
relationships: Inversely affected by Cortisol — high stress/cortisol suppresses lymphocytes. The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio reflects inflammatory balance. Low lymphocytes with elevated Cortisol suggests chronic stress or overtraining.
how_to_improve: Adequate sleep and recovery. Manage training load (avoid overtraining). Reduce chronic stress. Support gut health (70% of immune cells are gut-associated). Adequate protein and micronutrients (zinc, vitamin D, vitamin C).

name: Monocytes
description: White blood cells that differentiate into macrophages and dendritic cells, crucial for innate immunity and tissue repair.
why_it_matters: Monocytes are key players in inflammation, tissue repair, and pathogen defense. Elevated monocytes can indicate chronic infection, inflammatory conditions, or recovery from acute illness.
relationships: Part of the WBC differential. Elevated monocytes can accompany chronic inflammation (check hs-CRP). Monocyte-derived macrophages are involved in atherosclerotic plaque formation (connects to cardiovascular markers).
how_to_improve: Address underlying inflammation or infection. Anti-inflammatory diet and lifestyle. Monocyte count is primarily a diagnostic marker — focus on treating the cause of elevation.

name: Monocyte %
description: Percentage of white blood cells that are monocytes.
why_it_matters: Monocyte percentage provides context within the overall WBC differential. Relative elevation may indicate a shift in immune response type, even if absolute count is normal.
relationships: Interpret alongside absolute Monocyte count and total WBC. Relative percentages shift when other cell types change (e.g., neutropenia can make monocyte % appear high).
how_to_improve: Same approach as absolute monocyte count — address underlying causes of elevation or depression.

name: Eosinophils
description: White blood cells that combat parasites and are involved in allergic reactions and asthma.
why_it_matters: Elevated eosinophils suggest allergies, asthma, parasitic infections, or autoimmune conditions. They release inflammatory mediators that can damage tissues when chronically elevated.
relationships: Part of the WBC differential. Elevated eosinophils with elevated IgE suggest allergic disease. Can be associated with certain autoimmune conditions affecting other markers.
how_to_improve: Identify and manage allergic triggers. Treat parasitic infections if present. Reduce environmental allergen exposure. Anti-inflammatory approaches if autoimmune-related.

name: Eosinophil %
description: Percentage of white blood cells that are eosinophils.
why_it_matters: Eosinophil percentage helps contextualize eosinophil levels within the WBC differential. Elevation above 5% is clinically significant and warrants investigation.
relationships: Interpret alongside absolute Eosinophil count. High percentage can reflect true eosinophilia or simply a low total WBC making the proportion appear elevated.
how_to_improve: Same as absolute eosinophil count: address allergies, parasites, or autoimmune conditions.

name: Basophils
description: The rarest white blood cell type, involved in allergic reactions and inflammation by releasing histamine and heparin.
why_it_matters: Basophils play a role in allergic responses and certain inflammatory conditions. Significant elevation (basophilia) is rare but can indicate myeloproliferative disorders or severe allergic reactions.
relationships: Part of the WBC differential. Work with Eosinophils in allergic responses. Rarely elevated in isolation — usually part of a broader pattern.
how_to_improve: Basophil count is primarily diagnostic. Address underlying allergies or inflammation. Rarely requires targeted intervention.

name: Basophil %
description: Percentage of white blood cells that are basophils.
why_it_matters: Basophil percentage is typically very low (<1%). Elevation is uncommon and may indicate allergic conditions or myeloproliferative disorders.
relationships: Interpret alongside absolute Basophil count and total WBC. Very small numbers make percentage changes less reliable.
how_to_improve: Rarely requires intervention. Investigate if persistently elevated.

name: Neutrophil %
description: Percentage of white blood cells that are neutrophils.
why_it_matters: Neutrophil percentage reflects the balance between innate and adaptive immunity. High percentage can indicate bacterial infection or stress response. Low percentage may suggest viral infection or immune imbalance.
relationships: Inversely related to Lymphocyte percentage. The neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) is a powerful inflammatory and stress marker. Elevated by Cortisol and physical/emotional stress.
how_to_improve: Address underlying infection or stress. Manage cortisol through sleep, stress reduction, and avoiding overtraining. NLR >3 warrants attention.

name: Lymphocyte %
description: Percentage of white blood cells that are lymphocytes.
why_it_matters: Lymphocyte percentage reflects adaptive immune capacity. Low percentage with high neutrophil percentage suggests acute stress or bacterial infection. High percentage may indicate viral infection.
relationships: Inversely related to Neutrophil percentage. Used in neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR). Suppressed by Cortisol (chronic stress or overtraining).
how_to_improve: Ensure adequate rest and recovery. Manage stress. Support immune function with sleep, nutrition, and appropriate training loads.

name: ALT (Alanine Aminotransferase)
description: A liver enzyme released into the blood when liver cells are damaged. More specific to the liver than AST.
why_it_matters: ALT is the most specific liver enzyme. Elevation indicates liver cell damage from fatty liver (NAFLD), alcohol, medications, hepatitis, or intense exercise. Even mildly elevated ALT predicts metabolic and cardiovascular risk.
relationships: Typically rises with AST — the AST/ALT ratio helps distinguish causes (ratio >2 suggests alcohol; ratio <1 suggests NAFLD). Correlates with Insulin resistance and Triglycerides (fatty liver). Check with ALP and Albumin for complete liver panel.
how_to_improve: Reduce alcohol. Lose visceral fat (fatty liver is the most common cause). Avoid hepatotoxic supplements. Reduce fructose intake. Exercise regularly. Coffee consumption (2-3 cups/day) is protective. Note: intense exercise can transiently elevate ALT.

name: AST (Aspartate Aminotransferase)
description: An enzyme found in the liver, heart, muscles, and kidneys. Released into blood when these tissues are damaged.
why_it_matters: AST reflects liver damage but is less specific than ALT because it also comes from muscle. Intense exercise can elevate AST for 24-72 hours. The AST/ALT ratio provides diagnostic clues about the type of liver disease.
relationships: AST/ALT ratio <1: suggests non-alcoholic fatty liver. Ratio >2: suggests alcoholic liver disease. AST from muscle damage should be differentiated (check CK if recent intense exercise). Check alongside ALT, ALP, and Albumin.
how_to_improve: Same as ALT: reduce alcohol, lose visceral fat, avoid hepatotoxic substances. If elevated post-exercise, retest after 3+ days of rest. Address metabolic syndrome components.

name: Alkaline Phosphatase
description: An enzyme found in the liver, bones, kidneys, and intestines. Elevation can indicate liver or bone disease.
why_it_matters: ALP elevation with elevated ALT/AST suggests liver disease (especially bile duct obstruction). ALP elevation alone may indicate bone disease, vitamin D deficiency, or be normal in growing adolescents.
relationships: Elevated with GGT suggests hepatobiliary origin. Elevated without GGT elevation suggests bone origin. Check alongside Calcium, Vitamin D, and Phosphorus if bone disease suspected.
how_to_improve: Address underlying cause: if liver-related, same as ALT/AST optimization. If bone-related, ensure adequate Vitamin D, Calcium, and weight-bearing exercise. Young athletes may have physiologically elevated ALP from bone growth.

name: Albumin
description: The most abundant blood protein, produced by the liver. Transports hormones, drugs, and maintains fluid balance.
why_it_matters: Albumin is a marker of liver synthetic function and nutritional status. Low albumin indicates chronic liver disease, malnutrition, inflammation, or kidney disease (protein loss). It is also a predictor of overall health and longevity.
relationships: Low albumin falsely lowers Calcium readings (always correct calcium for albumin). Albumin drops during acute inflammation (negative acute-phase reactant) — check alongside hs-CRP. Reflects overall protein intake and liver health.
how_to_improve: Ensure adequate protein intake (0.8-1.2g/kg/day). Address liver disease if present. Reduce chronic inflammation. Albumin is a marker of overall health — improving general nutrition and reducing inflammation raises it.

name: Globulin
description: A group of blood proteins including immunoglobulins (antibodies), transport proteins, and clotting factors.
why_it_matters: Globulin levels reflect immune system activity and liver function. High globulin suggests chronic inflammation, infection, or autoimmune disease. Low globulin may indicate immune deficiency or liver disease.
relationships: Albumin/Globulin ratio helps assess liver and immune function. High globulin with low albumin suggests chronic inflammation. Check alongside Total Protein and hs-CRP.
how_to_improve: If elevated: address chronic inflammation or infection. If low: investigate immune deficiency. Globulin is primarily a diagnostic marker — treat the underlying condition.

name: Albumin/Globulin Ratio
description: The ratio of albumin to globulin, reflecting the balance between liver synthetic function and immune/inflammatory activity.
why_it_matters: A low A/G ratio suggests liver disease, chronic inflammation, or immune activation. It provides a quick assessment of the balance between nutritional/liver status and inflammatory/immune processes.
relationships: Low ratio: high globulin (inflammation) or low albumin (liver disease/malnutrition). High ratio: rare, may indicate immunodeficiency. Complements individual Albumin and Globulin values.
how_to_improve: Address the component that is abnormal: boost albumin through nutrition and liver health, or reduce globulin by addressing inflammation and infection.

name: Total Protein
description: Combined measurement of albumin and globulin in the blood.
why_it_matters: Total protein provides an overview of nutritional status, liver function, and immune activity. Deviations suggest liver disease, kidney disease (protein loss), malnutrition, or chronic inflammation.
relationships: Sum of Albumin and Globulin. Interpret with A/G ratio for more information. Low total protein with low albumin suggests malnutrition or liver disease. High total protein with high globulin suggests inflammation.
how_to_improve: Ensure adequate dietary protein. Address liver or kidney disease if present. Reduce inflammation if globulin is driving elevation.

name: Blood Urea Nitrogen
description: A waste product from protein metabolism, filtered by the kidneys. Reflects both protein intake and kidney function.
why_it_matters: BUN helps assess kidney function and protein metabolism. Elevated BUN can indicate kidney dysfunction, dehydration, high protein diet, or GI bleeding. Low BUN may suggest inadequate protein intake or liver disease.
relationships: BUN/Creatinine ratio helps distinguish causes: >20 suggests dehydration or GI bleeding. Check alongside Creatinine and eGFR for kidney function. High protein diets elevate BUN without kidney disease.
how_to_improve: If elevated: ensure adequate hydration, check kidney function (Creatinine, eGFR). If consistently high with normal kidneys, may reflect high protein intake (often not concerning). If low: ensure adequate protein intake.

name: Creatinine
description: A waste product from muscle metabolism, filtered by the kidneys. Reflects both muscle mass and kidney function.
why_it_matters: Creatinine is the primary marker for kidney function. Elevated levels suggest impaired kidney filtration. However, muscular individuals naturally have higher creatinine — always interpret with eGFR. Creatine supplementation also raises creatinine.
relationships: Used to calculate eGFR (estimated kidney filtration rate). BUN/Creatinine ratio provides additional diagnostic information. Muscular individuals and those taking creatine may have elevated creatinine with normal kidney function.
how_to_improve: If elevated with low eGFR: hydrate well, reduce excess protein if extreme, avoid nephrotoxic medications (NSAIDs), control blood pressure and blood sugar. If elevated but muscular/taking creatine, likely physiologic.

name: eGFR
description: Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate — calculated from creatinine, age, sex, and race. The best overall measure of kidney function.
why_it_matters: eGFR directly estimates how well the kidneys filter blood. Values below 60 indicate chronic kidney disease. Even mild reductions in eGFR increase cardiovascular risk. Important to track trends over time.
relationships: Calculated from Creatinine (can be falsely low in muscular individuals). BUN also reflects kidney function. Kidney disease accelerated by uncontrolled diabetes (HbA1c, Glucose) and hypertension.
how_to_improve: Stay well-hydrated. Control blood pressure and blood sugar. Avoid excessive NSAID use. Moderate protein intake if kidney disease is present. Exercise regularly. Address cardiovascular risk factors.

name: Sodium
description: The primary electrolyte maintaining fluid balance and blood pressure. Tightly regulated by the kidneys.
why_it_matters: Sodium is critical for nerve and muscle function, fluid balance, and blood pressure. Abnormal sodium is usually due to hydration status or hormonal regulation rather than dietary intake.
relationships: Regulated by aldosterone and ADH. Low sodium (hyponatremia) can occur with overhydration or adrenal insufficiency. High sodium suggests dehydration. Check alongside Potassium, Chloride, and CO2.
how_to_improve: If low: reduce excessive water intake, check adrenal function. If high: increase water intake. Sodium is tightly regulated — significant abnormalities require medical evaluation.

name: Potassium
description: Essential electrolyte for heart rhythm, muscle contraction, and nerve function.
why_it_matters: Potassium is critical for cardiac rhythm — both high and low levels are dangerous. It also affects muscle function, blood pressure regulation, and nerve signaling.
relationships: Inversely affected by aldosterone. Kidney function (eGFR, Creatinine) affects potassium excretion. Insulin shifts potassium into cells. Check alongside Sodium, CO2, and kidney markers.
how_to_improve: Bananas, potatoes, spinach, avocados, beans. Most adults need 2600-3400mg/day. If low: increase dietary sources or supplement (under medical guidance). If high: check kidney function and medications.

name: Chloride
description: An electrolyte that helps maintain fluid balance, blood pressure, and acid-base homeostasis.
why_it_matters: Chloride works with sodium and bicarbonate to maintain proper fluid balance and pH. Abnormal chloride usually reflects underlying acid-base or fluid disturbances rather than dietary issues.
relationships: Moves in parallel with Sodium in most conditions. The anion gap (Sodium - Chloride - CO2) helps diagnose metabolic acidosis. Check alongside Sodium, CO2, and kidney function.
how_to_improve: Chloride abnormalities typically reflect other conditions (dehydration, kidney disease, acid-base imbalance). Address the underlying cause rather than chloride directly.

name: Carbon Dioxide (CO2)
description: Bicarbonate level reflecting the blood's buffering capacity and acid-base status.
why_it_matters: CO2 (bicarbonate) maintains blood pH within a narrow range. Low CO2 suggests metabolic acidosis (from ketosis, kidney disease, or lactic acidosis). High CO2 suggests metabolic alkalosis.
relationships: Part of the electrolyte panel with Sodium, Potassium, and Chloride. Anion gap calculation uses CO2. Low CO2 with elevated BUN/Creatinine may indicate kidney disease causing acidosis.
how_to_improve: Address the underlying acid-base disturbance. Low CO2 from exercise-induced lactic acidosis resolves with recovery. Persistent abnormalities require investigation of kidney function or metabolic conditions.

name: Uric Acid
description: A waste product from purine metabolism. Elevated levels can cause gout and are associated with cardiovascular and metabolic disease.
why_it_matters: Elevated uric acid causes gout (painful joint inflammation) and is strongly associated with insulin resistance, hypertension, kidney disease, and cardiovascular risk. It is increasingly recognized as a modifiable metabolic risk factor.
relationships: Elevated by Insulin resistance (insulin reduces kidney uric acid excretion). Correlates with Triglycerides, HOMA-IR, and blood pressure. Fructose metabolism produces uric acid directly. Kidney function (eGFR) affects excretion.
how_to_improve: Reduce fructose (sugar, HFCS, fruit juice). Limit alcohol (especially beer). Reduce purine-rich foods (organ meats, shellfish) if elevated. Stay hydrated. Tart cherry extract may help. Address insulin resistance.

name: Total Bilirubin
description: A yellow pigment produced from red blood cell breakdown, processed by the liver and excreted in bile.
why_it_matters: Elevated bilirubin can indicate liver disease, bile duct obstruction, or increased red blood cell destruction. Mildly elevated bilirubin (Gilbert syndrome) is benign and may actually be protective as an antioxidant.
relationships: Elevated with ALT/AST suggests liver damage. Elevated with normal liver enzymes may indicate Gilbert syndrome or hemolysis. Direct vs indirect bilirubin helps distinguish hepatic from pre-hepatic causes.
how_to_improve: If due to Gilbert syndrome (common, benign): no treatment needed — mild elevation is actually associated with cardiovascular protection. If due to liver disease: address the underlying cause. Hydrate well.

name: GGT (Gamma-Glutamyl Transferase)
description: A liver enzyme particularly sensitive to alcohol intake and bile duct disease.
why_it_matters: GGT is the most sensitive liver enzyme for alcohol use and bile duct problems. It is also an independent predictor of cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and all-cause mortality, even at mildly elevated levels.
relationships: Elevated GGT with elevated ALP suggests bile duct pathology. GGT without ALP elevation suggests alcohol or medication effects. Correlates with oxidative stress, insulin resistance, and fatty liver. More sensitive than ALT for detecting early liver damage.
how_to_improve: Reduce or eliminate alcohol (most effective). Lose visceral fat. Address metabolic syndrome. Coffee consumption is protective. Avoid hepatotoxic medications and supplements. N-acetyl cysteine (NAC) supports glutathione and liver health.

name: Phosphorus
description: Essential mineral for bone formation, energy production (ATP), and DNA/RNA structure.
why_it_matters: Phosphorus is critical for cellular energy production and bone mineralization. Abnormal levels usually reflect kidney function, vitamin D status, or parathyroid disorders rather than dietary intake.
relationships: Inversely related to Calcium (PTH raises calcium while lowering phosphorus). Vitamin D increases both calcium and phosphorus absorption. Kidney disease impairs phosphorus excretion (check eGFR, Creatinine).
how_to_improve: Phosphorus deficiency is rare with adequate diet. If low: meat, dairy, nuts, legumes. If high: usually indicates kidney disease — address kidney function. Limit phosphorus additives in processed foods.

name: TIBC (Total Iron Binding Capacity)
description: Measures the blood's capacity to bind iron with transferrin. Reflects iron stores indirectly.
why_it_matters: TIBC rises when iron stores are low (the body makes more transferrin to capture available iron) and falls when iron stores are high. It helps distinguish types of anemia and iron disorders.
relationships: High TIBC with low Ferritin = iron deficiency. Low TIBC with high Ferritin = chronic disease or iron overload. Used to calculate Iron Saturation (Iron/TIBC x 100). Complements the iron panel.
how_to_improve: TIBC normalizes when iron status normalizes. If high (iron deficiency): increase iron intake. If low (iron overload): investigate hemochromatosis. TIBC is a diagnostic marker — treat the underlying iron abnormality.
